title: Freeze Feelings: An SEL Kernels Brain Game
description: Kids move around and freeze when an emotion is called out — a playful way to connect movement with feeling words.
source: UC Berkeley's Greater Good Science Center

content.overview: Children dance to music and freeze when it stops, striking a pose that shows a named emotion. Builds self-control and body awareness through play.
materials: Music to dance to (any upbeat songs work)
steps: Clear a space where your child can move without bumping into things.
Before each round, announce a feeling — happy, sad, angry, scared, embarrassed, etc.
Give them a moment to think about how they'd show that emotion with their face and body.
Start the music and let them dance freely.
Stop the music — they freeze in a pose that shows the emotion.
Play multiple rounds with different feelings.
content.tips: Model each feeling yourself, especially for younger children. Start with emotions they know well, then gradually introduce new ones. For a bigger challenge, have them hold the freeze pose for a count of five before relaxing.

title: Feelings Circle for Kindergarten
description: A short group routine where kids share how they're feeling using simple emotion words and pictures.
source: UC Berkeley's Greater Good Science Center

title: Feelings Circle for First Grade
description: A daily check-in circle where kids practice naming and sharing emotions with age-appropriate vocabulary.
source: UC Berkeley's Greater Good Science Center

title: Feelings Circle for Second Grade
description: Kids take turns naming how they feel and why, building emotional vocabulary and empathy through conversation.
source: UC Berkeley's Greater Good Science Center

title: Feelings Circle for Third Grade
description: A structured circle discussion where kids explore more nuanced emotions and what causes them.
source: UC Berkeley's Greater Good Science Center

title: Notes to Self for Kindergarten
description: Kids draw or dictate positive notes to themselves, noticing what makes them feel good and proud.
source: UC Berkeley's Greater Good Science Center

title: Notes to Self for First Grade
description: Kids write short encouraging notes to themselves, reflecting on their strengths and what they're learning.
source: UC Berkeley's Greater Good Science Center

title: Notes to Self for Second Grade
description: Kids practice self-compassion by writing kind, honest notes to themselves about who they are.
source: UC Berkeley's Greater Good Science Center

title: Notes to Self for Third Grade
description: A reflective writing activity where kids identify their strengths, challenges, and feelings honestly.
source: UC Berkeley's Greater Good Science Center

title: Emotional Intelligence Playbook
description: A research-backed guide with simple at-home strategies for helping kids understand and manage their emotions.

title: Feelings Check-In
description: A quick worksheet where kids circle or draw how they're feeling right now — great as a daily habit.

title: The Worry Scale: Coping with Anxiety and Fear
description: Kids learn to rate how big their worries feel on a scale, helping put anxious feelings in perspective.

title: Feelings Wheel
description: An interactive emotion wheel that helps kids find the exact word for what they're feeling beyond just 'sad' or 'mad'.

title: Chameleon Moods: Identifying Emotions Activity
description: Using a chameleon story, kids explore how moods can shift and learn to name the feelings behind the change.

title: Cool Down Corner
description: Set up a calming space at home where kids can go to name big feelings and use simple tools to self-regulate.

title: Different Emotion Words Within One Emotion
description: Kids discover that 'mad' can mean annoyed, furious, or disappointed — expanding their emotional vocabulary.

title: Big Emotions vs. Small Emotions Worksheet
description: Kids sort feelings into 'big' and 'small' buckets, building awareness of emotional intensity.

title: Match the Emojis: Feelings Faces
description: A printable matching game where kids pair emoji faces to emotion words — simple and engaging for any age.

title: The Feelings Song
description: A simple, catchy song that helps young kids memorize and express a range of emotions through music.

title: Body Language: The Telephone Game
description: Kids pass an emotion through body language only — no words allowed — to practice reading nonverbal cues.

title: Nonverbal Communication Puppet Show
description: Kids use puppets to act out emotions without speaking, focusing on facial expressions and gestures.

title: Virtual Temperature Checks
description: Kids mark where they are on an emotional thermometer — a fast visual tool for checking in on feelings.

title: Two Word Check-In
description: Kids describe how they're feeling in exactly two words — a fast, low-pressure daily check-in routine.

title: Check-In Circle for Community Building
description: A structured go-around where each person shares how they're feeling — builds trust and emotional openness.

title: What's My Temperature?
description: Kids use a thermometer metaphor to rate and communicate how 'hot' or 'cool' they feel emotionally in the moment.

title: Emotion Charades
description: Kids take turns acting out emotions for others to guess — a fun, active way to recognize and name feelings.

content.overview: Children take turns acting out emotions using only body language and facial expressions — no words — while others guess what feeling is being shown.
materials: A bowl or hat to draw from
steps: Write different emotions on slips of paper and put them in a bowl.
Take turns being the actor — draw an emotion from the bowl without showing anyone.
Act out the emotion using only your face and body (no words or sounds).
Family members try to guess which emotion is being shown.
After each round, talk about which body language cues gave it away.
content.tips: Remind everyone that there's no single right way to show an emotion — some people are naturally more expressive than others. Extend the activity by asking 'What would make you feel better when you feel that way?'

title: Emotion Portraits
description: Kids draw faces showing different emotions, then talk about what makes each expression unique.

title: Mood Diary
description: Kids track their moods daily in a simple journal, helping them spot patterns in how and why they feel different things.

title: Self-Awareness and Advocacy Card
description: Kids fill out a card about their strengths, challenges, and what kind of support helps them most.

title: Tense and Relax
description: Kids squeeze and release different muscle groups to feel the difference between tension and calm in their bodies.

content.overview: Children squeeze and release different muscle groups one at a time, learning to notice the difference between tension and calm in their own bodies.
steps: Neck (Turtle): Push shoulders up toward ears, hold for 3 counts, then slowly relax for 3 counts. Repeat.
Hands (Lemon): Squeeze hands into tight fists, hold for 3 counts, then slowly open and relax. Repeat.
Stomach (Fence): Squeeze your belly in like you're squeezing through a narrow fence, hold for 3 counts, then relax. Repeat.
Feet (Mud): Push feet down into the floor, hold for 3 counts, then slowly relax. Repeat.
Arms (Push-Pull-Drop): Sitting in a chair, grip the edges. First push down, then pull up against the seat, then drop your arms to your sides. Repeat.
Between each exercise, ask: 'How does that body part feel now compared to when it was tight?'
content.tips: If your child feels any discomfort, invite them to just sit quietly and breathe instead. Once they know the routine, let them lead you through it — kids love being in charge.
